authoritative_sources.title: National Operations & Maintenance Scope of Work
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GSA is an authoritative federal property operator. Its 2023 O&M specification defines work-order classes, CMMS recordkeeping, reporting, emergency versus urgent handling, and subcontractor proposal expectations relevant to building maintenance planning.

authoritative_sources.title: Checklist for the Routine Inspection of Buildings
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This official GSA checklist defines systematic building-inspection and preventive-maintenance recordkeeping, keeping the lane tied to inspection-informed planning rather than ad hoc failure response.

authoritative_sources.title: Operations & Maintenance Best Practices Guide: Release 3.0
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. Department of Energy Federal Energy Management Program
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: DOE FEMP is an authoritative federal facilities-operations source. Its O&M guide defines maintenance planning, CMMS tracking, preventive or predictive maintenance framing, and operational-efficiency vocabulary used for capacity and prioritization analysis.

authoritative_sources.title: Communication and Coordination for Host Employers, Contractors, and Staffing Agencies
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OSHA is the primary U.S. workplace-safety regulator. This guidance explicitly requires host employers and contractors to coordinate planning and scheduling to minimize impacts on safety and to communicate changing hazards before work proceeds.
used_for: refusal when safety conditions change the answer

authoritative_sources.title: Maintaining Your Property
authoritative_sources.publisher: New York City Department of Buildings
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: A city building department is an authoritative local building regulator. This guide ties maintenance duties to construction-code compliance and routes boiler and plumbing work to licensed individuals and permit processes, defining where this lane must stop.
used_for: licensed boiler and plumbing boundary handling
refusal of unsupported code and permit judgments

authoritative_sources.title: Special Inspections
authoritative_sources.publisher: Seattle Department of Construction and Inspections
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This official inspection-program guidance gives a concrete public baseline for special inspection nomination, field reports, final letters, and the requirement that final letters be accepted before permit completion.

authoritative_sources.title: Importance of Involving Key Support Staff in Facility Design through Commissioning and Turnover
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: WBDG is a recognized public building-guidance platform used across federal facilities practice. This turnover reference ties closeout quality to O&M documentation, training, BIM or COBie data, and maintenance-readiness evidence.

workflow_stages.name: Demand intake and maintenance-frame normalization
workflow_stages.description: Classify incoming maintenance demand, normalize facility, asset, trade, permit, and requested completion context, and confirm that the request is specific enough for planning review.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance

workflow_stages.name: Constraint and capacity assessment
workflow_stages.description: Assess crews, subcontractors, access windows, service levels, shutdown dependencies, permits, inspections, and asset criticality before recommending timing or allocation.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Site-readiness, permit, and inspection gating
workflow_stages.description: Determine whether work can be planned, scheduled, or represented as complete based on permit state, approved plans, inspection status, special inspection letters, safety posture, and jurisdiction-specific prerequisites.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance

workflow_stages.name: Allocation, tradeoff, and escalation packaging
workflow_stages.description: Generate recommendation-only sequencing and allocation options, then escalate unresolved approval, safety, licensed-trade, or closeout conflicts instead of treating them as planning decisions.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance

artifacts: work orders and preventive or reactive backlog records
permit records, approved plans, and shutdown coordination notes
inspection records, special inspection field reports, and final letters
site logs, safety observations, and access approvals
crew calendars, subcontractor commitments, and service-level targets
change orders, deficiency logs, test reports, and installation evidence
turnover packages, O&M documentation, and training records
systems_of_record: approval matrices and tenant policy libraries

allowed: Classify planning work as in scope, out of scope, or blocked on missing prerequisites.
Assess whether available records support a bounded timing, routing, booking, or allocation recommendation.
Produce evidence-backed schedule recommendations, prioritization outputs, constraint logs, and approval-needed summaries without claiming final approval authority.
escalate: Required permit, inspection, site, schedule, or tenant-policy records are missing or materially conflict.
A safety deficiency, failed inspection, permit condition, or closeout dependency changes whether work may proceed or be treated as complete.
The request requires final staffing, budget, permit, code, licensed-trade, superintendent, or operational approval authority.
refuse: Commit final schedule approval, overtime approval, staffing approval, or service-level degradation approval.
Approve permits, inspections, code compliance, or licensed trade work without explicit authority.
Plan work as ready when permit, inspection, safety, or closeout blockers remain unresolved.

domain_failure_modes: planning work without confirmed permit status, approved plans, or associated shutdown prerequisites
committing labor or subcontractor windows without verified capacity, access, or service-level state
allowing schedule pressure to override a failed inspection, changed hazard, or unresolved special-inspection condition
treating apparent field completion as final while closeout and turnover evidence remains incomplete
inventing tie-break rules when competing priorities lack an approved decision rule
collapsing facilities maintenance planning into generic backlog management without documenting permit, inspection, and site dependencies
acting as code interpreter, licensed trade authority, or final approver instead of escalating to the correct owner

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Request that implies licensed trade or code-interpretation authority
Demand exceeds capacity or approved service levels
Conflicting priorities with no approved tie-break rule
Stakeholder asks for final approval the lane cannot grant
Tradeoffs require approval outside lane authority

unresolved_ambiguities.impact: Permit issuance, inspection nomination, and closeout sequencing vary by AHJ and must be tenant-supplied at runtime.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Tenant-specific tie-break rules and service-level tolerances
unresolved_ambiguities.impact: Exact priority matrices, blackout windows, overtime policy, and degradation thresholds must be provided by the tenant before deterministic prioritization.
